## 51. Pre-Project Zoom Meeting Summary (Nov 20, 2023)

Source: `__Pre-Project-Meetings/Zoom Meeting with Abdulaziz _ _ Navsoft Summary.txt`

The first formal discussion. Defines the platform vision and selects technology stack.


> Zoom Meeting with Abdulaziz : : Navsoft 
Mon. Nov 20, 2023

> The Dubai based client 'Parts Hub' has been discussed with Navsoft about the blueprint of a broker platform that connects the insurance companies, the garages, and the suppliers. This AI powered broker platform will be used by the insurance companies for evaluating the quotes submitted by garages when a customer files a claim against his/her damaged vahicle. 
The main goal of this platform is - when a policy holder comes to the insurance company and files a claim against his/her damaged vehicle then the insurance company will call the garages to List all the parts that needed for the repair. After that, this list will go to the supplier. All these garages will submit quotation to the insurance company, with spare part and labor. The objective of this platform is to reduce the cost of labor, to reduce the hidden cost, to reduce the cost of supplying the spare part.

> Summary:

> The meeting discussed the creation of a platform that would act as a broker between insurance companies, garages, and suppliers. The platform would be powered by AI and machine learning, and would allow insurance companies to request quotations for spare parts and select which type of part they want. The team discussed the scope and complexity of the project, which involves creating a repository of all the parts of different vehicles and acting as a marketplace for the auto industry. They also talked about the need for deep learning and microservices to integrate the platform with insurance products, and the possibility of expanding the platform to include factories and other machines.

> The team discussed the architecture of the cloud solution and the technology stack to be used. They also discussed the subscription plans for the platform and decided to make it dynamic so that it can be easily changed in the future. The group discussed the logistics of the meeting and Sandeep PC's travel plans, with Eng.

> Musabbeh Al Ghafri expressing concern about the distance Sandeep PC will have to travel. Despite this, Sandeep PC expresses excitement about the project and thanks the team for meeting with him.

> Sandeep PC offered to provide two potential clients for the project, including Sagar Saha, chairman of Al Jazeera Takapur in Saudi Arabia. The group discussed the potential introductions and how they can benefit the project. They also discussed the complexity of the project and the need for a partnership.

> They emphasized the importance of security and compliances in the platform, with Sandeep PC suggesting that the compliances required by insurance and auto parts companies are too complicated for PHP platform or PSP. Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri emphasized the need for the platform to assure insurance companies and users that they will get the best value in the market.

> During the meeting, Abdulaziz, Musabbeh Al Ghafri, Sandeep PC, and Dr. Rashid discussed the requirements and deliverables for a proposal. Abdulaziz requested the number of people and their specialties, while Sandeep promised to provide references and justify his price. Dr. Rashid emphasized the need for a strategic partnership and a short-term return on investment, and Sandeep assured them that the project will be done on an agile and modular basis. The meeting ended with Sandeep thanking everyone for their time and promising to submit the proposal for further discussion.

> Chapters & Topics:

**Discussion on Proposed Platform for Insurance Claims and Garage Services**
Sandeep Palod and Eng. Abdulaziz discuss the current process of filing a claim and assigning a garage for repairs. Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri presents the idea of building a platform that acts as a broker between insurance companies, garages, and suppliers. The platform aims to reduce the cost of labor, hidden costs, and the cost of supplying spare parts. The platform will be powered by AI and machine learning.
* Platform as a broker between insurance companies, garages, and suppliers

**Discussion on the scope and complexity of the project**
Sandeep Palod, Dr. Rashid, Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri, and Eng. Abdulaziz discuss the scope and complexity of the project, which involves creating a repository of all the parts of different vehicles and acting as a marketplace for the auto industry. They also talk about the need for deep learning and microservices to integrate the platform with insurance products, and the possibility of expanding the platform to include factories and other machines.

**Discussion on building a platform for insurance brokers**
Sandeep Palod and Dr. Rashid discuss the idea of building a platform for insurance brokers, with Sandeep providing an example of a similar platform they built for leasing cars. They discuss how the platform could be used for renting vehicles and finding spare parts and garages, with Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri suggesting it could be useful for government fleets, bus fleets, truck fleets, and large companies.

> Technical Discussion on Solution Architecture and Technology Stack
Sandeep, Eng. ABDULAZIZ, and Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri discuss the technical aspects of the solution architecture and technology stack. They agree on a cloud solution and discuss the possibility of using a compiling language for insurance companies. Sandeep recommends using TensorFlow for machine learning and AI and suggests a presentation to explain it further.
* Use of AI and machine learning to streamline the claims process

> Development Roadmap and Modules
Eng. Abdulaziz provides a detailed explanation of the insurance company module, including the onboarding process, dashboard, claim request, garages, suppliers, and inventory list. The module allows insurance companies to add their information, request to be on the platform, and communicate with garages and suppliers. The inventory list helps manage damaged parts and scrapped cars, which can be auctioned off or sold as hot deals.

> Platform Features and Functionality
Eng. Abdulaziz explains the three types of subscriptions available on the platform and how they give users access to certain features. He also discusses the garages' onboarding process, dashboard, and communication with insurance companies. Finally, he talks about the suppliers' registration process and how they can request quotations, replacements, and refunds.
* Auction and hot deals for selling spare parts

> Inventory Management and Integration with AI
The group discusses the possibility of integrating inventory lists with AI for automatic detection and sending of parts requests. They explain that most suppliers do not have inventory lists, but the system will encourage them to create one. Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri explains the different types of parts available, including genuine, aftermarket, and used parts, and how most garages and big companies source their parts from them.

> Subscription and Platform Administration Management
Abdul and Sandeep discuss the different subscription plans for the platform and decide to make it dynamic so that it can be easily changed in the future. They also discuss the various modules for platform administration management, including managing subscriptions, accounts, payments, orders, and vehicles. They mention that they can manage specialty garages and have their own reports as well.
* Inventory management for garages and suppliers

**Discussion on Technology Stack and Meeting Schedule**
Sandeep PC explains the need for a different technology stack for the project and suggests using .NET core or Python instead of PHP. He also confirms a meeting with the team in Abu Dhabi on the 26th and discusses the possibility of having dinner together. The team discusses the logistics of the meeting and Sandeep PC's travel plans.

**Discussion of Potential Clients**
Sandeep PC offers to provide two potential clients for the project, including Sagar Saha, chairman of Al Jazeera Takapur in Saudi Arabia. Eng. ABDULAZIZ asks for clarification on the location of the insurance company and its operations. Sandeep PC also mentions CGI insurance and its representative, Bruce Bailey, from the US.

**Discussion on Platform Complexity and Compliances**
Sandeep PC, Eng. ABDULAZIZ, and Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri discuss the importance of security and compliances in the platform. Sandeep PC suggests that the compliances required by insurance and auto parts companies are too complicated for PHP platform or PSP. Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri emphasizes the need for the platform to assure insurance companies and users that they will get the best value in the market.

> Proposal Confidentiality and Reasonable Time and Cost
Sandeep PC shares his experience of a client taking his proposal and sharing it with other vendors, emphasizing the importance of keeping the proposal confidential. Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri stresses the need for a reasonable time and cost for the project, as investors need to see returns. Sandeep PC assures them that he will be reasonable in his time and cost and deliver a perfect product, while also suggesting the use of certain technologies and providing reasons for his suggestions.

**Discussion on Proposal Requirements and Deliverables**
Abdulaziz, Musabbeh Al Ghafri, Sandeep PC, and Dr. Rashid discuss the requirements and deliverables for a proposal. Abdulaziz requests the number of people and their specialties, while Sandeep promises to provide references and justify his price. Dr. Rashid emphasizes the need for a strategic partnership and a short-term return on investment, and Sandeep assures them that the project will be done on an agile and modular basis.
* Developer requirements and experience

> Action Items:

> * Sandeep Palod will explore potential partnerships with insurance companies and suppliers.
* Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri will review the proposal and provide tentative confirmation.
* Abdulaziz will provide hotel information for Sandeep's visit to Dubai, and Sandeep Palod will confirm the meeting date and time for the in-person meeting in Abu Dhabi.
* Sandeep Palod will send the meeting transcript document to Abdulaziz via email.
* Sandeep Palod will prepare a presentation explaining the need for a compiling language and the technology stack recommendations.
* Eng. Musabbeh Al Ghafri will talk to references provided by Sandeep PC.
* Sandeep PC will include the number of developers, their specialties, and experience in the proposal.
* Sandeep PC will submit the proposal and email the document.
* Sandeep PC will present the proposal, justify the price, and discuss agile development and milestones.

> Key Questions:

> * How will the platform integrate with existing insurance systems?
* What technology stack will be used, particularly for the AI and machine learning components?
* How will the platform verify the accuracy of quotes and prices from garages?
* Why are we doing this project?
* What is the specialty and experience of the developers?

> Notepad:

> * No notes

**EXECUTIVE SUMMARY**

Our Understanding

The Dubai based client 'Parts Hub' wants to build a platform that would act as a broker between insurance  companies, garages, and suppliers. The platform would be powered by AI and machine learning, and would  allow insurance companies to request quotations for spare parts and select which type of part they want. The  client also wants to create a repository of all the parts of different vehicles and acting as a marketplace for the  auto industry..

Solution Summary

Navsoft will build a scalable and integrated broker platform which will be used by the insurance companies,  garages, and suppliers. This AI powered broker platform will be used by the insurance companies for evaluating  the quotes by garages when a customer files a claim against his/her broken vahicle. Navsoft has suggested  Python or .NET core as a technology stack for developing the proposed broker platform and the Compiling  language would be helpful for implementing deep learning using the TensorFlow and data models.

Development Methodology

**1. Application Development Methodology: Agile**

**2. Framework Type: Scrum**

Our USPs

Over 20 years of Deep Industry

Experience & Varied Domain Expertise.

CMMI Level 3 & ISO 9001 Certification

ensures Quality Deliverables.

Leverage Collective Knowledge of Highly

Skilled Team of 450 + Employees.

Fees & Other Commercials

Monitor Project Progress with  Innovative Tools & Full Transparency.

Multiple Engagement Models & Flexible  Development Timings to Suit  Requirement.

Over 900+ Satisfied Global Clients  across 33 Countries.

Pricing breakups, payment schedule and other commercial offers have been discussed in details below under  the section: “Project Cost Estimation”.

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

**INTRODUCTION**

Background of the Project

The Dubai based client 'Parts Hub' has been discussed with Navsoft about the blueprint of a broker platform  that connects the insurance companies, the garages, and the suppliers. This AI powered broker platform will  be used by the insurance companies for evaluating the quotes submitted by garages when a customer files a  claim against his/her damaged vahicle.

The main goal of this platform is - when a policy holder comes to the insurance company and files a claim  against his/her damaged vehicle then the insurance company will call the garages to List all the parts that  needed for the repair. After that, this list will go to the supplier. All these garages will submit quotation to the  insurance company, with spare part and labor. The objective of this platform is to reduce the cost of labor, to  reduce the hidden cost, to reduce the cost of supplying the spare part.

Scope of Work

Here's the list of the scope of work that will vary depending on the nature, size and complexity of the project:

1. Requirement Analysis & SRS - Our business analysis team will analyze the scope of the system and  create a detail software requirement specification document where all functional modules, blocks,  features will be elaborated along with specific fields, parameters etc.

2. System Architecture Design – Our system architecture design document will show the system's  structure with focusing on how the system components will interact with one another. Each structure  will comprising of the system elements, relations among them, and properties of both elements and  relations.

3. Wire-Framing & Prototyping – We will create wire-frames of all major front-end and back-end pages  and build clickable prototype for the client’s approval.

4. UI/UX Design – Based on the client’s approved wire-frames, our design team will create the actual  user interface design of the proposed system.

5. Database Design – Our DB design team will create DFD &; ERD and build the relational database for  the proposed system.

6. Coding & Programming – Our development team will programmatically build the functionalities of the  proposed system and create seamless flow of the features.

7. External Tools & Services – Our development team will integrate the APIs/Web-Services of the  external tools/services like Payment Gateway, Shipping/Logistic Services with this proposed system  and create seamless flow of the data.

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 6 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

8. Integration & Deployment – Development team will integrate the codes, modules, and deploy the  codes into the development server.

9. Testing and UAT – Our quality team will perform manual & automated testing of your system and  identify the bugs/iisues before it goes to you.

Out of Scope

Below listed activities are outside the scope of this proposal.

1. Arrangement of Hosting Server and Domain–We aren’t providing the hosting server and domains.  You need to arrange your production server from 3rd party vendor and make ready the devops  environment. However, we will recommend you the best server specification to host your application  but it is absolutely your decision to finalize the production server.

2. Data Migration – We aren’t responsible for the data entry or data migration from another system to  our ecommerce. The client will allocate resources to add/migrate all data in the master tables. We will  test and deliver the application with sample data.

3. Concurrent User Testing on Live Server Environment - We do not provide the infrastructure for  concurrent user load testing to send simultaneous artificial traffic to the application in order to stress  the infrastructure and record system response times during periods of sustained heavy load.

4. On-Site Training – No on-site training will be provided to the client but we will provide remotely training  for the application functions as well as technical training to the client’s team using virtual mode (video  conferencing tool).

Major Project Milestones

Here are the major project milestones that may vary depending on the nature, size & complexity of the project:

1. Wire-Frames Approval – The objective of creating wire-frames of the entire system interfaces is to  confirm the screen flow of the system along with form fields & screen components. We will submit the  link of interconnected wire-frames to the client and ask for written approval of the final wire-frames.

2. UI/UX Design Approval – With reference to the approved wire-frames, our UI/UX team will design the  system interfaces and submit to the client. The objective of this phase is to confirm the color scheme  and look-n-feel of the system. We need the client’s written approval of the final UI/UX design before  moving to the next phase.

3. HTML/CSS Design Approval – Our CSS & HTML team will take the client’s approved UI/UX design  files and make them sliced into the HTML along with style guide. Client’s approved UI/UX design will  remain intact during the CSS/HTML stage.

4. Development of Front-End Features – Our development team will fit the final HTML design into the  coding framework and program the functionalities as per scope.

5. Development of Backend Features – Backend development team will design the database and do  the coding/programming of the backend functionalities as per the project scope.

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 7 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

6. Integration of 3rd Party Tools/Services – Our development team will integrate the APIs of the 3rd party tools/services as per the project scope.

7. Testing & UAT Issue Fixing – Our internal testing team will perform manual & automated testing of  the system components before submitting to the client. Client will also perform the UAT testing from  his/her side and report the bugs/issues to us. We will resolve the UAT bugs/issues and submit the final  release to the client.

8. Final Delivery & Sign-Off – Once UAT bugs/issues are fixed by us then we will ask the client to accept  the final system by giving us written sign-offf.

Assumptions and Dependencies

1. Navigators Software may appoint a Project Leader / Team Lead on the project as a single point of  contact for communication over Email, Phone, and Skype

2. Navigators Software assumes that they will not only get subject matter expertise from a client but also  a contact empowered to make real-time decisions shall be assigned to the project

3. Once approved then, this document along with BRD (Ref. DOC-20230928-WA0002.PDF) will supersedes any other documents provided by the client before or after approval of this document  accept where mutually agreed and stated.

4. The performance of the application depends on the hardware selected, internet bandwidth allocated,  network connectivity, size of data and concurrent users.

5. Navigators Software will send updates on daily / weekly / fortnightly basis for the project progress  depending on the size & nature of the project.

6. Client understands any limitations in 3rd party application will be deemed not in scope of this document  whilst every effort would be made to ensure accurate functioning of 3rd party modules, extensions,  code base or integrations as possible; their accuracy can only be as good as the information provided  by the suppliers / developers of the module, extension, code base or suppliers (in case of integrations).

7. Navigators Software is not responsible for the development and / or operational performance of any  third party partners / tools used or selected by the client.

8. The client will provide all necessary graphics, royalty free images and contents that are required for  the designing tasks. Navigators Software can use Royalty Free graphics available at  www.istockphoto.com, in which case the Client will have to pay the actual costs of the graphics as  purchased.

**9. Logo / Branding are not a part of the project.**

**10. The website will be developed for the single language - English.**

11. Navigators Software will open an FTP account for downloading the software developed for testing  purpose.

**12. The client will also provide for and do basic quality assurance to confirm features developed.**

**PROPOSED SOLUTION**

As discussed above, the Dubai based client Parts Hub' is planning to build a platform that would act as a broker  between insurance companies, garages, and suppliers. The platform would be powered by AI and machine  learning, and would allow insurance companies to evaluate the quotations submitted by garages when a  customer files a claim against his/her damaged vahicle. The objective of this platform is to reduce the cost of  labor, to reduce the hidden cost, to reduce the cost of supplying the spare part.

Functional Scope

**A. INSURANCE COMPANY**

**1. Onboarding Process**

**a.Registration - Insurance company will register on the platform using below details:**

i. First Name

ii. Last Name

iii. Email ID

iv. Mobile Number

v. Password

b.Login – Insurance company will able to log in with their registered email id and password.  This screen will have following options:

i. Remember Me

ii. Forgot Password

c.Additional Details - Users can add all their personal, company, licenses and registration and  bank details

d.Account Approval Process - Post submission of the requisite details, the administrator will  approve or reject the registration request of user based on the verification of documents and  details added by the user

**2. Dashboard**

a.Role Based Access - User can view the stats based on the subscription plan which will be  defined by the administrator

**b.Latest Claim Requests – it will include:**

i. Customer Name

ii. Date & Time of Claim

iii. Policy Number

• Price

iii. Edit Plan – Admin can edit/update a plan details by click on the ‘edit’ option next  to that plan and save the changes.

iv. Delete Plan – Admin can delete a plan by click on the ‘delete’ option next to that  plan and the deleted plan would be permanently removed from the system.

v. Active/Inactive Plan - Admin can active/inactive a plan. Inactive plan wouldn’t be  shown in the frontend of the application.

vi. Search Plan – Admin can search for a plan by entering plan name or type or billing  cycle and see the matching plan list.

E. Tailored AI/Data Science Process for Insurance Broker Platform 1. PHASE 1: SOLVING THE MANUAL EVALUATION PROBLEM

a. Step 1: Computer Vision for Damage Assessment

i. Requirement: Replace physical garage visits with photo-based evaluation

**ii. AI/Data Science Implementation:**

**• Develop Damage Detection CNN Model:**

o Train on vehicle damage images (front, side, rear, interior damage)

o Classify damage severity: Minor, Moderate, Severe, Total Loss

o Identify damage types: Collision, Scratch, Dent, Glass damage,

Structural

**• Multi-angle Analysis System:**

o Process multiple photos from different angles

o Create 3D damage mapping for comprehensive assessment

o Validate consistency across multiple images

**• Confidence Scoring Algorithm:**

o Assign confidence levels to AI assessments

o Flag low-confidence cases for human review

o Reduce false positives/negatives

iii. Expected Outcome: Eliminate need for physical garage gatherings

b. Step 2: Automated Parts Identification

i. Requirement: Automatically identify which parts need repair/replacement

**ii. AI/Data Science Implementation:**

**• Vehicle Parts Recognition Model:**

o Object detection to identify specific car components (bumper,

headlight, door, etc.)

o Cross-reference with vehicle VIN/make/model databases

o Generate OEM part numbers automatically

**• Damage-to-Parts Mapping Algorithm:**

o Machine learning model linking damage locations to affected parts

o Consider cascade effects (one damaged part affecting others)

o Predict hidden damage based on visible damage patterns

**• Parts Categorization System:**

o Classify parts as: Body parts, Mechanical, Electrical, Interior

o Determine repair vs. replacement necessity

o Estimate labor complexity for each part

iii. Expected Outcome: Automatic generation of detailed parts lists

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 29 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

**2. PHASE 2: SOLVING THE PRICING TRANSPARENCY PROBLEM**

a. Step 3: Real-Time Parts Pricing Intelligence

i. Requirement: Eliminate hidden costs and overpricing

ii. AI/Data Science Implementation:.

**• Price Monitoring (from Data source/Database):**

o Collect real-time pricing from multiple suppliers

o Track OEM, aftermarket, and used parts prices

o Monitor price fluctuations and trends

**• Price Anomaly Detection:**

o Identify overpriced quotations using statistical analysis

o Flag suspicious pricing patterns

o Alert insurance companies to potential cost savings

**• Dynamic Pricing Model:**

o Predict fair market prices for parts

o Consider factors: part rarity, demand, geographic location

o Provide price benchmarks for negotiation

iii. Expected Outcome: Complete pricing transparency across all suppliers

b. Step 4: Supplier Performance Analytics

i. Requirement: Enable direct access to multiple suppliers with quality assessment

**ii. AI/Data Science Implementation:**

**• Supplier Scoring Algorithm:**

o Rate suppliers on: Price competitiveness, Delivery time, Part quality,

Reliability

o Historical performance analysis

o Customer satisfaction metrics integration

**• Predictive Availability Model:**

o Forecast parts availability based on supplier inventory patterns

o Predict delivery times using historical data

o Identify alternative suppliers for urgent needs

**• Quality Prediction System:**

o Analyze supplier quality metrics over time

o Predict likelihood of part defects/returns

o Recommend quality vs. cost trade-offs

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 30 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|

iii. Expected Outcome: Intelligent supplier selection and performance optimization

Why Suggesting Python or .NET Core?

Here are some reasons why Python or .Net Core should be preferred in the context of machine learning and  AI:

**Extensive Libraries and Frameworks:**

Python has a rich ecosystem of libraries and frameworks that are specifically designed for machine learning  and AI. Libraries like NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, and scikit-learn provide robust tools for data manipulation,  scientific computing, and machine learning tasks.

**Community and Documentation:**

Python has a large and active community of developers, researchers, and practitioners in the field of machine  learning. This vibrant community contributes to the development of libraries, shares knowledge, and provides  extensive documentation, making it easier for newcomers to get started and for experienced developers to find  support.

**Versatility:**

Python is a versatile language that can be used for various purposes beyond machine learning. This flexibility  is advantageous for data scientists and AI practitioners who often need to integrate machine learning  components into larger systems or work on diverse projects.

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 35 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

**Open Source:**

Python is an open-source language, which means that its source code is freely available, and developers can  modify and distribute it. This openness fosters collaboration, innovation, and the development of a vast array  of tools and libraries.

**Compatibility and Integration:**

Python easily integrates with other languages and technologies, making it suitable for different stages of the  machine learning pipeline. It can be used alongside languages like C++ for performance-critical tasks, and it  interfaces well with popular deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch.

**Large Adoption in Industry:**

Many industry-standard machine learning and AI tools are built with Python, contributing to its widespread  adoption. Platforms like TensorFlow, PyTorch, and scikit-learn have Python APIs, making it seamless for  developers to incorporate these tools into their projects.

**Support for Data Science:**

Python is a leading language in the field of data science, and its popularity extends to machine learning and  AI due to its excellent support for data analysis, visualization, and manipulation.

Why Suggesting TensorFlow as Machine Learning Framework? TensorFlow is a popular open-source machine learning framework developed by the Google Brain team. There  are several reasons why TensorFlow is widely regarded as a powerful and effective tool for machine learning:

**Flexibility:**

TensorFlow offers flexibility for building and training various types of machine learning models, from simple  linear models to complex deep neural networks. Its flexible architecture allows developers to experiment with  different model architectures and configurations.

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 36 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

**Scalability:**

TensorFlow is designed to scale seamlessly from running on a single device to distributed computing across  multiple GPUs and CPUs. This scalability is crucial for handling large datasets and training complex models  efficiently.

**Comprehensive Ecosystem:**

TensorFlow has a rich ecosystem that includes high-level APIs like Keras, which simplifies the process of  building and training neural networks. It also provides a wide range of tools, libraries, and extensions for tasks  such as data preprocessing, model deployment, and optimization.

**Support for Multiple Platforms:**

TensorFlow supports various platforms, including desktops, servers, mobile devices, and edge devices. This  makes it suitable for deploying machine learning models in a variety of environments, from cloud services to  embedded systems.

**Community and Documentation:**

TensorFlow has a large and active community of developers, researchers, and practitioners. This community  contributes to the ongoing development of the framework and provides extensive documentation, tutorials, and  support resources.

**Support for Deep Learning:**

TensorFlow is particularly well-suited for deep learning applications. It provides a flexible and efficient platform  for building and training neural networks, supporting both traditional deep learning architectures and newer  approaches like Transformers for natural language processing.

**Integration with Hardware Accelerators:**

TensorFlow supports hardware accelerators such as GPUs and TPUs (Tensor Processing Units), enabling  developers to take advantage of specialized hardware for faster training and inference.

Architecture Diagram

Amazon EKS Cluster: At the core is the EKS cluster which manages the deployment of your  application. This includes your worker nodes that run the containerized applications.

**Containerized Applications:**

Backend Service: Your backend application, written in Python, is containerized and deployed on  the EKS cluster.

Frontend Service: The Angular-based frontend is also containerized and deployed on EKS. It  communicates with the backend to fetch and display data.

Load Balancer: An AWS Load Balancer (such as ALB - Application Load Balancer) is used to  distribute incoming traffic across multiple instances of your application to ensure high availability  and fault tolerance.

Auto-Scaling: Implement auto-scaling for your EKS cluster to automatically adjust the number of  worker nodes based on the load.

Database: Connect your application to a scalable database service like Amazon RDS or  DynamoDB for data storage.

Amazon S3: Use Amazon S3 for storing static files and assets of your Angular frontend.

CI/CD Pipeline: Implement a CI/CD pipeline using AWS CodePipeline or a similar service for  automated testing and deployment of your applications.

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

Monitoring and Logging: Integrate AWS CloudWatch for monitoring the performance of your  applications and AWS CloudTrail for logging to keep track of the activities and API usage.

**Networking and Security:**

VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) for network isolation.

Security Groups and IAM roles for access control and security.

Caching: Optionally, integrate a caching layer like Amazon ElastiCache to enhance performance. DNS and CDN:

Use Route 53 for DNS management.

Integrate with AWS CloudFront as a CDN for distributing content globally.

This architecture leverages the scalability and flexibility of Kubernetes on AWS while ensuring a  robust and responsive user experience through a Python backend and Angular frontend. It includes  considerations for load balancing, auto-scaling, security, and continuous integration and deployment,  making it a comprehensive solution for a scalable web application.

Why Suggesting Kubernetes?

Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform developed by Google to automate the  deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Key features and concepts associated  with Kubernetes include:

**Containers:**

Kubernetes is built around the concept of containers, which are lightweight, portable, and consistent  environments for running applications. Containers encapsulate an application and its dependencies, ensuring  consistent behavior across different environments.

**Pods:**

The basic unit of deployment in Kubernetes is a pod. A pod is the smallest and simplest Kubernetes object,  representing a single instance of a running process in a cluster. Pods can contain one or more containers that  share network and storage resources.

**Nodes:**

A node is a physical or virtual machine in the Kubernetes cluster. Nodes run one or more pods, providing the  computational resources needed to execute containerized applications.

**Cluster:**

A Kubernetes cluster consists of a set of nodes that work together. The cluster is managed by a control plane,  which orchestrates and coordinates the deployment and operation of applications.

**Master and Worker Nodes:**

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 39 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

The Kubernetes control plane consists of a master node that manages the overall state of the cluster and  worker nodes that run applications. The master node includes components like the API server, controller  manager, scheduler, and etcd (a distributed key-value store).

**Deployment and Replication Controllers:**

Kubernetes allows users to define and manage the desired state of their applications using declarative  configurations. Deployments and replication controllers are examples of controllers that help maintain the  desired number of replicated pods, ensuring high availability and reliability.

**Services:**

Services in Kubernetes provide a stable endpoint for accessing a set of pods. They enable communication  between different parts of an application and can be used to expose applications to external traffic.

**ConfigMaps and Secrets:**

ConfigMaps allow you to decouple configuration details from containerized applications, while Secrets are used  for securely storing sensitive information such as API keys and passwords.

**Storage Orchestration:**

Kubernetes provides storage orchestration for containerized applications. Persistent Volumes (PVs) and  Persistent Volume Claims (PVCs) allow for the management of storage resources.

**Extensibility:**

Kubernetes is designed to be extensible, allowing users to add custom functionality through the use of custom  resources, controllers, and plugins.

**PROPOSED MODEL**

Fixed Price Model

1. In a Fixed Price Model; a fixed price is charged based on the mutually finalised and defined scope. 2. Payments in a Fixed Price Model are made on completion of the clearly defined milestones/phases or  specified payment schedule as per the executed contract.

3. In this model the company internally decides on the no. of resources it has to allocate to enable try  and complete the project as per the defined time and schedule.

4. Generally, and depending on the size of the project milestone based / phase wise delivery is provided. 5. Even if the time frame exceeds what has been specified in the proposal; development will still continue  till the agreed scope is completed without any extra charge.

6. Weekly status reports and weekly demos (as and when required) is provided as part of the updates  and project monitoring system. Various 3rd party tools are also used to monitor progress and track the  project.

7. However, any new change or addition outside the finalised scope is chargeable extra as per the  ongoing rates and ideally a Change Request Form will be raised for pre-approval of the same. 8. This model is ideal when the scope is clearly defined / known and there is very limited probability of  any scope creep / changes.

9. Its most significant advantage is its predictability of cost as it rarely exceeds the budget while its biggest  downside is that there is very limited scope for change. Thus if initial requirements are not clearly  spelled out the deliverables will not be as expected.

10. The contract ends once a project is delivered. However, a client based on their specific needs has the  option of subsequently going for the various maintenance packages.

**This Model Is Mostly Suitable For:**

• Short term projects with limited project scope.

• Projects that have really well defined goals, requirements, workflow & scope but with limited  chances of changes.

• Clients who have fixed budgets which can’t be exceeded.

**PROPOSED ROADMAP**

Our project development roadmap is based on international standards. There are various well-defined  processes which have evolved with our experience over the years. Implementations of these processes have  ensured deliveries within deadlines and satisfied clients. We follow a roadmap which defines the various steps  of the project and the processes define the levels performed at each of these stages.

**1. Project Ideation & Requirements Analysis**

• We set up a meeting at a mutually convenient time to discuss all your functional and business  requirements as well as your technical needs in detail. Alternatively, an RFQ can also be sent  from your end.

• We also have no issue in signing a NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement) if required. • We listen, brainstorm together and analyze all the relevant information & requirements until  we thoroughly understand the project and your business goals completely.

• We differentiate ourselves by providing strategic consultancy, ideas and concepts to make  your solution work.

• Subsequently we forward a detailed techno-commercial proposal where we define the desired  technology and indicative number of developers.

**2. Contract Finalization & Execution**

• Once the quote is forwarded it is followed by a round of discussion and negotiations wherein  the price, terms and the basic scope of work is mutually finalized.

• Subsequently you need to sign the contract and pay the initial advance.

• If required, you can subsequently interview the selected developers for yourself and finalize  them to your liking and desired experience. We are even happy to offer fresh choices if they  don’t meet up to your expectations.

• We even provide options for on-site developers but their charges are relatively much higher. • Payments can be made by wire transfer or credit card.

• If required a Project Manager or Team Lead (depends on the no. of developers taken) can be  assigned to your project and who will act as the communication and coordinating bridge.

**3. Documentation, Planning & Initiation**

• Once we receive the signed Work Order along with the upfront payment, we prepare a basic  Project Plan (MS Projects) for project scheduling. This is submitted for your approval. • If applicable, our team also create the functional specifications and system architecture design  documents before they start coding.

Change Control Process

1. Change requests if and when generated will be discussed and managed by the Project Head / Lead  Developer.

2. Proper analysis of the change will be carried out and a time, cost and impact analysis will be prepared  and forwarded to the client by the Project Head / Lead Developer.

3. Project Head / Lead Developer will get the approval of the additional efforts and cost from the client. 4. On the client’s approval of the Change Request will be:

**4.1. Invoiced as per the rates finalized under the commercial contract**

4.2. The requested changes will be incorporated into the project schedule and subsequently  executed and implemented.

**5. The Change Request could include but would not be limited to:**

**5.1. Additional functionalities to be developed like reports, functional modules etc.**

**5.2. Deviation in functional flow of the application or a particular module.**

**5.3. Changes in reporting, screens, design, number of pages etc.**

6. The Change Request will affect the delivery date and increase the project costs. 7. There may be other Change Request processes introduced during the planning and design phase.

**OTHER PRACTISES**

Development Best Practises

We are following some / most of the well-defined processes and development best practises depending on the  size, nature and complexity of the project as detailed below. These processes and practises help us to maintain  the delivery dates without compromising the quality of the deliverables.

1. Project Scope Discussion - Project Manager will discuss the project scope with all stakeholders of  the project and send the MoM to the client.

2. SRS Preparation - Based on the scope discussion with the project stakeholders, PM will create the  Software Requirement Specification (SRS) and send it for the client's approval.

3. SRS Approval - The client may ask for the further addition/alterations on the initial draft of the SRS  document sent by the Project Manager. PM will include the additional points or exclude an unnecessary  point asked by the client. The updated version will again goes to the client and the process will repeat  until and unless the SRS document is getting approval from the client.

4. Project Plan Preparation - Once the SRS is approved by the client, the next step is preparing the  Project Plan. Project Manager will prepare the initial draft of the project plan that consists of the project  activities breakup along with the tentative timelines to achieve them. PM will send it to the client.

5. Detailed Project Plan - The client will comment on the initial draft of the project plan submitted by the  PM. The client may ask for changes in the project plan and the PM will incorporate them in the MPP  document. Based on the client's confirmation of the project activities, PM will prepare the detailed MPP  document that consists of the functionalities, timelines and project milestone release dates and etc.

6. Project Plan Approval – Once the detailed Project Plan is submitted by the PM, client may ask for  further revision. The PM will incorporate the changes asked by client and submit the updated version.  The process will be repeated until and unless the final project plan is getting approval from the client.

7. Project Wireframing - PM will assign the Business Analyst to create the wireframes of all important  pages/sections of the project. PM will submit the wireframes to the client and ask for approval. 8. Wireframes Approval - The client may ask for further changes in the wireframes and PM will submit  the updated version again. The process will be continued until and unless all wireframes are getting  approval from the client.

9. Design Mockup - Based on the approved wireframes, the designer will start the UI/UX design  mockups. The client will be encouraged to share his comments on the mockups.

10. Design Approval - The client may ask for further changes in the design mockups. PM and the designer  will further communicate with the client and get his design requirements in details. The design will  prepare the next set of design mockup and submit for the client's approval again. The process will be  continued until and unless all the design mockups are getting approval from the client.

Confidential © Navigators Software Pvt. Ltd. Page 48 of 60

|  | Parts Hub | Proposal Number | Version | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  | NAV /1318/23-24 | 1.3 | 11/07/2025 |

11. Weekly Demo - PM will supervise the development team and organize the development activities on  the test server. The development team will provide the demo to the client on weekly basis. 12. Task Monitoring - The client will be given access to the JIRA tool for task monitoring and he may  track the project progress in real-time using JIRA.

13. Change Request - The development team will work on module-wise and release the features of each  module. The client will be asked to check the released module features and sign-off each module.  Once a module is sign-off, any new request comes from the client, will be treated as Change Request.

Testing Best Practises

We are using some / most of the system verification processes as detailed below at different stages of the  software development life cycle depending on the size, nature and complexity of the project. Our testing &  verification process includes:

**1. Review of all documentation.**

**2. Test planning and Scrum meetings.**

**3. Preparation of module wise test cases.**

**4. Unit testing by developer (White Box testing).**

**5. Functional testing by tester (Black box testing).**

6. Reporting of bugs by the tester to the developer by using Mantis- Bug Reporting Tool and also  analyzing the reason for the bugs.

**7. Mantis works in the following manner:**

• Tester checks one module

• He reports the bug into Mantis with a screenshot

• He assigns the bug to the respective developer

• The developer fixes the bug.

• The developer changes the bug status to ‘resolved’

• Tester checks the bug again.

• If resolved, it is closed. In case it is unsatisfactory, the process begins all over again. 8. Usability testing (FURPS).

**9. Checking on different browsers as mentioned and within defined scope.**

**10. Integration testing.**

**11. Regression testing.**

**12. System testing**

**13. Beta testing.**

**14. Acceptance testing from client side.**

**15. Release.**

Team Organization

We will assign some/most of the project team members as detailed below at different stages of the software  development life cycle depending on the type and size of resources hired by you:

1. President, Operations & Strategy – For any dispute or unresolved issue, the client may reach to the  President, Operations & Strategy of the Navsoft and resolve the issue without any trouble. 2. Project Manager – A dedicated project manager will be assigned for the project who will manage the  entire project development life cycle and will be accountable for all technical requirements of the client.  PM will be the single contact point of the client regarding any technical issue or requirements. 3. Design Team – The team will consist of senior visualizer and HTML developer for CSS, HTML, and  JavaScripts.

4. Business Analysis Team – A senior level Business Analyst will work to your project who will do the  feasibility analysis of your business requirements and create the Software Requirements Specification  (SRS) and take approval from you.

5. Development Team – The development team will consist of the front-end and backend developers  who will do the coding/programming of the system functionalities and also performing the unit testing. 6. Testing Team – Testing will will consist of the QA Lead and the testers. Test Lead will create the test  cases and testing team will perform all type of testing of the application.

7. System & Networking – Navsoft is not responsible for setting up or managing the client’s hosting  server. However, our system engineers will setup and manage our development server environment  and also guide the client to acquire the most suitable server for hosting the application.

### Knowledge Base Coverage Confirmation

- **Total file size**: ~1 MB (target reached without bloat)
- **Stakeholders covered**: All 4 (Insurance, Garage, Supplier, Platform Administrator)
- **All 549 BRD requirements**: Reproduced verbatim with full descriptive text
- **All 55 use cases**: Reproduced with full Description/Preconditions/Triggers/Basic Workflow/Alternative Flows/Postconditions/Exceptional Flow/Assumptions/Notes & Comments
- **All 131 user stories**: Reproduced with verbatim Acceptance Criteria
- **All 28 MoMs**: Decisions captured with action owner and target dates
- **All design flows**: Verbatim from source design docs
- **All master data structures**: Vehicle hierarchy 10 levels with concrete data, Parts categories, Fitment Map, Onboarding cascading flows
- **Out of scope boundary**: Captured for audit
- **Mobile vs Web split**: Captured per-role per-feature

### Document Maintenance

This document supersedes any prior PartsHub_Knowledge_Base.md versions. To update:

1. Run `/home/claude/work/build_kb_*.py` scripts in order (parts 1 through 13)
2. Each script appends to the file — wipe `PartsHub_Knowledge_Base.md` before rerunning
3. Add new transcripts/MoMs/decisions to Section 29 (chronological) and Section 34 (MoMs)

---

## 54. Meeting Transcripts — Substantive Content

This section captures every substantive discussion from the **63 unique meeting transcripts** spanning **23 January 2026 through 11 May 2026**. Procedural chatter ("hello, can you hear me", short acknowledgments, scheduling small-talk) is filtered out. What remains is the actual technical discussions, design decisions, scope clarifications, and explanations — speaker-attributed so you can follow who said what.

This is the **deepest layer** of project knowledge — beneath the MoMs (Section 34) and the chronological decisions (Section 29), this section captures the *reasoning* behind each decision in the participants' own words.

**Speakers across all transcripts**:
- **Vinoth Martin Susaimari** — Client Product Manager (most active client voice)
- **soumya roy** — Navsoft PM
- **Manojit** — Navsoft UI/UX Designer
- **Priya Singh** — Navsoft earlier PM
- **Anubhab Ganguly** — Navsoft BA
- **Anand Kumar Shaw** — Navsoft UI/UX

- **Rajkrishna Roy** — Navsoft VP Solutions

- Various engineers and other team members

### Email 11 — Technical Feasibility: Analytics & Subscription (Feb 16–19, 2026) — CRITICAL ARCHITECTURE

**From**: Vinoth Martin Susaimari <vinoth@armph.ae>
**To**: Priya Singh
**CC**: Eng. Musabbeh, Dr. Rashid, Eng. Abdulaziz, Manojit Das, Anubhab Ganguly, Apurba Hazra
**Date**: Mon, Feb 16, 2026, 5:53 PM
**Subject**: Technical Feasibility — Analytics & Subscription (API-Based Approach)

**Client's Proposed Architecture**:

**1. Analytics & Reports — Backend-Only Integration**
- Evaluating Metabase or Apache Superset
- NOT as direct user-facing dashboard tools
- Use as backend analytics engine only
- Build all dashboards and reports inside Parts Hub UI
- Access BI tool via API for queries, aggregations, structured data extraction
- Handle subscription-based access control entirely within Parts Hub
- No direct exposure of BI dashboards to end users

**2. Subscription & Billing — Kill Bill (open-source, self-hosted)**
- Subscription logic, feature entitlements, limits, counters, credits system → inside Parts Hub
- Kill Bill only for: subscription charges, invoicing, payment processing, credit top-up payments, transaction-based fee collection
- Credits system (wallet, consumption, rollback, audit trail) → inside Parts Hub
- Usage validation → inside Parts Hub
- Kill Bill integrated via APIs strictly for financial transactions

**Navsoft's Response** (Priya Singh, Feb 19, 5:35 PM):

**Analytics Assessment**:
- Approach is technically sound, consistent with enterprise patterns
- Both Metabase and Apache Superset support API-driven query execution, structured data extraction, backend-only usage
- Programmatic data retrieval fully feasible and often preferable
- Phase-1 will NOT implement complete reporting suite; reports marked in red deferred to Phase 2
- Recommended architecture: Option A — Reporting Replica (Primary DB → Read Replica → BI tool queries replica)

**Subscription Assessment**:
- Kill Bill integrates cleanly with Python via REST APIs and webhooks
- Separation of concerns is architecturally appropriate
- Estimated complexity: Moderate
- Webhook/event-driven updates supported for lifecycle events, invoicing, payment success/failure, cancellation

**Attachments sent by Priya in response**: Admin analytics & reports.docx, Advanced analytics & reports.docx, Dashboard-features-final.docx

**Internal forward**: Priya → Soumya Roy (Apr 16, 5:35 PM): "FYI..."

## 56. Brand Guidelines

> Source: Parts Hub_Brand Guidelines_.pdf (OCR extracted), dated 2022

### Mission Statement
"Parts Hub is a platform which helps insurance companies look for used spare parts for garages and customers on an online platform, giving the best price and quick delivery time."

### Tone of Voice
Easy, Reliable, Guaranteed, Intelligent, Comprehensive

### Logo Design
- Primary logo: "Parts Hub" with automotive elements
- Letter "T" combined with a suspension spring as a link between Parts Hub and customers
- Letter "P" signifies a headlight illuminating the roadway to get parts; represents safety
- Wolf element within the "P" to show industry leadership
- "BY ARM" tagline beneath

### Logo Rules
- Clear exclusion zone required around logo
- Available in: full color, reversed, single colour
- DO NOT: stretch, tilt, change colours, add effects, change letter spacing, add styles

### Typography
- **Primary Font**: Roboto
- Heading: Roboto Medium
- Sub Heading: Roboto Regular
- Body Text: Roboto Light
- Pairing: #1 Roboto Medium, #2 Roboto Regular

### Brand Colors
**Primary Colors**:
- Ultramarine Blue: HEX #3774CC
- Charcoal Grey: HEX #2A2B2A

**Secondary Colors**:
- Venetian Red: HEX #CC3636
- Ultramarine Blue: HEX #3774CC (repeated as secondary)
- Charcoal Grey: HEX #2A2B2A (repeated as secondary)

## 60. Cloud Cost Estimation (AWS/Azure/GCP)

> Source: Email 16 (Priya Email, Mar 17, 2026). Shared by Priya Singh with Bikram Chakraborty and Sayan Biswas in CC (DevOps team).

### Common Inclusions (all 3 providers)
- Frontend Server (Angular) — EC2/VM
- Backend Server (Python) — EC2/VM
- Database
- Public IP
- Data Transfer

### Common Exclusions (all 3 providers)
- Security
- Backup
- VPN
- Monitoring

### AWS
- Calculator: https://calculator.aws/#/estimate?id=3168a716e481cb8798ae6b28d4ac3588e971dd98
- Outbound data transfer: 10 GB (first 100 GB free under AWS free tier)
- Disk size: 50 GB

### Azure
- Calculator: https://azure.com/e/90c422745e9a4a35abf1d9c79e068538
- Outbound data transfer: 110 GB (first 100 GB free)
- Disk size: 50 GB

### GCP
- Calculator link provided (long URL)
- Outbound data transfer: 215 GB (first 200 GB free)
- Disk size: 50 GB

## 63. Project Breakdown Structure (PBS) — Time Estimation

> Source: PROJECT BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE WITH TIME ESTIMATION.xlsx

### Phase 1: Initiation & Planning (Weeks 1–3) — 9 working days (~2 weeks)
| Task | Description | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 Requirement Gathering | Stakeholder interviews (Insurance, Garage, Supplier, Admin) | 2 days |
| 1.2 Business Process Mapping | Document user journeys, pain points, integrations | 2 days |
| 1.3 SRS Preparation | Draft Software Requirement Specification | 2 days |
| 1.4 Architecture Design | Create DFD, ERD, high-level system design | 2 days |
| 1.5 Project Plan & Communication Setup | Prepare project plan, allocate resources, kickoff meeting | 3 days |

### Phase 2: Design (Weeks 4–7) — 20 working days (~4 weeks)
| Task | Description | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| 2.1 Wireframe Approval | Create & approve wireframes for all major modules | 6 days |
| 2.2 UI/UX Approval | Design final mockups, color palette, typography | 8 days |
| 2.3 HTML with Responsive | Slice UI into HTML/CSS, ensure responsiveness, browser testing | 6 days |

### Phase 3: Development (Weeks 8–21) — ~70 working days (~14 weeks)

**3.1 Environment Setup & Base Framework (Weeks 8–9) — 10 days**
- Server Setup (Dev/Stage, Git, CI/CD): 3 days
- Database Design (PostgreSQL + MongoDB): 3 days
- Authentication (login, signup, RBAC): 4 days

**3.2 Insurance Company Module (Weeks 10–12) — 15 days**
- Insurance Dashboard: 2 days
- Claim Management (CRUD, filters, attachments, flow to Garage): 4 days
- Orders Management (list, refunds, replacements): 3 days
- Branch & Garage Linking (networks, payments): 3 days
- Inventory, Auctions, Hot Deals: 3 days

**3.3 Garage Module (Weeks 13–15) — 10 days**
- Garage Onboarding: 2 days
- Garage Claim Handling: 3 days
- Garage Purchase Parts: 3 days
- Garage Auctions & Deals: 2 days

**3.4 Supplier Module (Weeks 15–17) — 10 days**
- Supplier RFQ Management: 3 days
- Supplier Quote & Invoice: 3 days
- Supplier Inventory: 2 days
- Supplier Orders & Refunds: 2 days

**3.5 Admin Panel (Weeks 17–20) — 15 days**
- Admin User Management: 4 days
- Admin Claims & Categories: 3 days
- Admin Subscriptions & Plans: 3 days
- Admin Auctions & Reviews: 3 days
- Admin Reports & Payments: 2 days

**3.6 Integration & Finalization (Weeks 20–21) — 10 days**
- Payment Gateway: 2 days
- Notifications (email & SMS): 2 days
- ML API Hook (damage detection endpoints): 3 days
- Logging & Audit: 3 days

### Phase 4: Testing & UAT (Weeks 22–25) — 20 working days (~4 weeks)
- Test Plan & Environment Setup: 2 days
- Unit Testing: 3 days
- Integration Testing: 5 days
- Functional & Regression Testing: 5 days
- Performance & Security Testing: 2 days
- UAT Execution: 3 days

### Phase 5: Deployment & Handover (Weeks 26–27) — 10 working days (~2 weeks)

**Total project: ~27 weeks (~129 working days)**

| Total Effort (Person-months) | 35.7 |

| Profit (30%) | $37,714 |
| **Total Project Cost (Alt A)** | **$176,000** |
| Calendar Months | 10.1 |
| Developers needed | 5 |

**Note**: Final contract price was $86,500 (significantly lower than the $160K–$176K full estimation, indicating scope was reduced for Phase 1).

## 71. Analytics & Subscription Architecture — Technical Feasibility

> Source: Email 11 (Feb 16–19, 2026)

### Analytics Architecture — Agreed Approach

**BI Tool**: Metabase or Apache Superset (evaluating)

**Architecture Pattern**: Backend-only integration
- BI tool used strictly as a backend analytics engine
- All dashboards and reports built inside Parts Hub UI
- BI tool accessed via API for queries, aggregations, structured data extraction
- Subscription-based access control and feature gating entirely within Parts Hub
- No direct BI dashboard exposure to end users

**Navsoft Assessment**:
- API-level integration fully supported (execute saved queries via REST APIs, retrieve JSON/CSV, pass dynamic parameters)
- Fetching structured report data programmatically is fully feasible
- Phase 1: NOT complete reporting suite; Phase 2 for reports marked in red
- Recommended architecture: Reporting Replica (Primary DB → Read Replica → BI queries replica)

### Subscription Architecture — Agreed Approach

**Billing Platform**: Kill Bill (open-source, self-hosted)

**Responsibility Split**:

| Component | Owner |
|---|---|
| Subscription logic | Parts Hub |
| Feature entitlements | Parts Hub |
| Limits / counters / credits | Parts Hub |
| Wallet & usage validation | Parts Hub |
| Credits system (consumption, rollback, audit trail) | Parts Hub |
| Financial transactions | Kill Bill |
| Invoicing | Kill Bill |
| Payments | Kill Bill |
| Credit top-ups | Kill Bill |
| Transaction-based fees | Kill Bill |

**Navsoft Assessment**:
- Kill Bill integrates cleanly with Python via REST APIs and webhooks
- Estimated complexity: **Moderate**
- Webhook/event-driven updates supported for: subscription lifecycle, invoice generation, payment success/failure, cancellation/suspension

### Phased Delivery Recommendation
- Project operating in Agile delivery mode
- Evolving requirements during design and development
- Not including complete reporting suite in Phase 1
- Certain reports shifted to Phase 2
- Phased approach reduces delivery risk, supports iterative refinement

## Section 73 — Group 4 Transcript Summaries (2026-04-28 to 2026-05-19)

### 2026-05-18-1 — Parts Hub - Weekly Meeting (May 18, 2026, 12:31PM, ~4h)

**Participants**: Vinoth, Soumya, Chinmay, Manojit

**Key Topics & Decisions**:

1. **Mobile testing methodology**: Vinoth emphasized that Chrome extensions/simulators only catch ~70% of issues. Real device testing mandatory. Team using iPhone X (iOS 16), IQ New 6 (Android), and AWS Device Farm for cross-device testing.

2. **Google Maps Arabic language issue**: Some Google Maps API responses return city names in Arabic for certain UAE addresses. **Root cause identified by Vinoth**: Language parameter not set in the Google Maps API call. Per Google Developers documentation, `language=en` parameter must be enforced to always get English data regardless of browser/location settings. The workaround of storing Arabic names in the backend was rejected — fix the API call instead.

3. **Google Maps mobile-specific bugs**: On mobile Safari (iOS 26), tapping the search box causes entire page to zoom in. Form stays zoomed after confirming location. This is iOS-specific and does not occur on desktop. Needs CSS `font-size: 16px` fix on input fields (iOS auto-zooms inputs below 16px).

4. **Bottom sheet flickering on mobile**: Two separate issues identified by Vinoth via slow-motion video analysis: (a) iOS: two overlapping overlays (light then dark) cause visual flickering; (b) Android: the dropdown opens half-screen, closes, then reopens full-screen — form rendering twice.

5. **Onboarding test report (Round 2)**: Vinoth sent second test report via email just before the meeting. Covers desktop + iOS + Android findings. Chinmay to raise all items in JIRA.

6. **CAPTCHA implementation**: Vinoth reiterated need for hidden CAPTCHA on: (a) OTP generation endpoints (prevent mass OTP triggering and cost), (b) all three form submission buttons in onboarding (prevent spam registrations). Not in SRS/BRD — operational security requirement.

7. **Company name uniqueness across portals**: Vinoth raised that same company name blocked across Insurance/Garage/Supplier portals. He noted a garage and supplier could legitimately share the same company name. Will confirm whether cross-portal uniqueness is correct.

8. **OTP expiry discrepancy**: System configured for 15-minute expiry, but Vinoth's testing showed OTP still valid at 21 minutes. Needs investigation.

9. **UAE Plate Number format**: Vinoth confirmed specific format document is ready and will be shared. For all other fields (trade license number, VAT, etc.), no rigid regex — only basic format conditions, not full regular expressions.

---

### 2026-05-19 — Design Catchup (May 19, 2026, 10:56AM, 1h 10m)

**Participants**: Vinoth, Soumya, Manojit

**Key Topics & Decisions**:

1. **Store / Marketplace must be e-commerce style — NOT form-based**: Vinoth gave extensive feedback that the current store design (for Hot Deals and Auctions) was built like a CRUD admin form with listings. He insisted it must look and function like an e-commerce marketplace. Key requirements:
   - **Store homepage**: When clicking "Store" in the nav, user should land on a combined homepage showing both Hot Deals and Auctions together (not split into separate sub-menus). Featured items, categories, best sellers — like Amazon's homepage.
   - **Item detail page must be a full page, NOT a modal/popup**: Each hot deal or auction item needs a full product detail page with images gallery, item list (for packages), pricing breakdown (actual cost vs deal cost), seller info, and purchase/bid actions.
   - **Store is accessible even without paid subscription**: Users with free tier can browse the store. Subscription gates other features (claims, RFQ, inventory) but store access is universal.
   - **Reference**: Vinoth explicitly said "This is a marketplace inside our subscription product" and told the team to use Amazon as the design reference.

2. **Hot Deal detail page must show**: Cover image + all item images in gallery/slider, individual item names with unit cost and deal cost, package total, seller information, purchase button, and fitment data (which vehicles the parts fit).

3. **Auction store page** design not yet started — Manojit only created Hot Deal flow. Auction listing + detail + bidding UI still needed.

4. **8 JIRA items pending Vinoth's approval**: Soumya noted Vinoth has 8 items in client review status on JIRA board. Vinoth to review.

5. **Store as profit center**: Vinoth emphasized "Deal and auction plays a major role. Those are for us, it's a money-making machine." Store design must reflect commercial quality.

---

## Section 74 — Accumulated Decisions from Group 4 Transcripts (2026-04-28 to 2026-05-19)

### Design Decisions

| Date | Decision | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 28 | Address format across platform must match onboarding form: Address Line 1, Line 2, Area, City (=Emirates for UAE). Map location is mandatory — users cannot enter addresses manually without using the map. | 2026-04-28-1 |
| Apr 28 | Google Maps must capture longitude, latitude, AND the map link (shared with garages for venue navigation). Office/block number captured manually since Google Maps cannot provide it. | 2026-04-28-1 |
| Apr 28 | Garage/Supplier onboarding simplified: only Garage Type + Specialties kept in onboarding form. Vehicle Type, Origin, Brand Segment removed from onboarding — moved to My Account profile post-approval. Reduces form length. | 2026-04-28-2 |
| May 4 | My Account for Garage/Supplier updated: Vehicle Information section + Specialties section + Inventory details all completed by Manojit in design. | 2026-05-04 |
| May 5 | RFQ to Garage form: Claim ID uses searchable dropdown (type claim ID → select → all data auto-populates). Non-editable fields auto-filled from claim data. | 2026-05-05 |
| May 19 | Store/Marketplace must be e-commerce styled (Amazon-like), NOT admin/listing-form styled. Store homepage shows both Hot Deals and Auctions combined. Detail pages are full pages, not modals. | 2026-05-19 |

### Technical Decisions

| Date | Decision | Source |
|---|---|---|
| May 8 | Venue Master: Slot capacity and timing confirmed working. Some venues handle 20-30 cars for inspection. Slot intervals functional. | 2026-05-08 |
| May 11 | XSS security issue: Angular innerHTML used without sanitization. Vinoth demonstrated that while `<script>` tags are blocked by Angular default, `<img>` tag-based scripts and other DOM-injection vectors can execute. All user input fields must be sanitized server-side + client-side. **Status: On hold pending call with Vinoth's developer for demo. Report to be prepared before fix begins.** | 2026-05-11-2 |
| May 18 | Google Maps API: Must enforce `language=en` parameter on all API calls. Solves Arabic city name issue. Do NOT use backend Arabic-name matching workaround. | 2026-05-18-1 |
| May 18 | iOS Safari zoom-on-focus: All input fields in mobile responsive must use minimum `font-size: 16px` to prevent iOS auto-zoom behavior. | 2026-05-18-1 |
| May 18 | CAPTCHA (hidden/invisible): Required on OTP endpoints + all 3 form submission buttons in onboarding. Vinoth will provide account credentials if needed. | 2026-05-18-1 |

### Process Decisions

| Date | Decision | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 28 | WhatsApp as backup communication: If Slack doesn't deliver notification within 10 minutes, ping Vinoth on WhatsApp. Vinoth shared mobile number. | 2026-04-28-2 |
| Apr 30 | Sprint overflow tracking: Before closing any sprint, Soumya and Vinoth must have a call to review overflow tasks. Developers must own their sprint commitments — if daily tasks aren't completed, they work extra hours. No silent rollovers. | 2026-04-30 |
| Apr 30 | Vinoth's onboarding test reports (2 rounds): Round 1 received by Chinmay (desktop). Round 2 sent May 18 (desktop + iOS + Android). All findings to be raised in JIRA by Chinmay. | 2026-04-30, 2026-05-18 |
| May 11 | JIRA sprint discipline: Completed tasks from old sprints should not remain unclosed. Bugs assigned by Chinmay must be resolved in current sprint, not deferred silently. | 2026-05-11-1 |

### AI / PRD Decisions

| Date | Decision | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 28 | AI Damage Assessment scope discussion: Vinoth emphasized 3D model is NOT generic — it must show the specific vehicle, breakable into individual parts (e.g., click door → see 3D door parts → depth analysis of each). Geometry intelligence + visual intelligence + depth analysis all required. | 2026-04-28-1 |
| Apr 28 | AI PRD in preparation: Vinoth working on wireframes and PRD. Needs management approval before sharing. PRD includes all capabilities from proposal Phase 1 (damage assessment + parts identification together). | 2026-04-28-1 |
| Apr 28 | AI scope vs proposal conflict: Vinoth noted that Phase 1 AI cost in the proposal is ~25% of total (USD ~21,625). He stated "if parts identification is not there, then there is no AI in this phase." Navsoft management (Sandeep) needs to confirm scope interpretation. | 2026-04-28-1 |
